title: Osteoarthritis
oneLiner: Degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive cartilage loss and bony overgrowth, most common in elderly and obese, leading cause of chronic disability worldwide.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 68-year-old woman with a BMI of 34 presents with gradual-onset right knee pain over 2 years. Pain is worse with walking, climbing stairs, and at the end of the day. She has “start-up” stiffness lasting about 10 minutes in the morning. On examination, you find bony enlargement at the medial joint line, palpable crepitus during flexion‑extension, mild effusion, and quadriceps wasting. She has a slight varus deformity. There is no warmth or erythema.

**Dissected pattern clues:**
- **Age > 60** → cartilage senescence + cumulative mechanical stress; OA peaks after 60.
- **Gradual onset** → chronic degenerative process, not acute inflammatory.
- **Activity-related pain** → mechanical loading exacerbates cartilage friction.
- **Short morning stiffness (< 30 min)** → distinguishes from inflammatory arthritis (RA > 60 min).
- **Bony enlargement** → osteophytes (bone spurs) at joint margins.
- **Crepitus** → rough articular surfaces grinding; felt or heard during movement.
- **Varus deformity** → medial compartment collapse from asymmetric cartilage loss.
- **No warmth/erythema** → absent systemic inflammation; OA is ‘cold’ arthritis.
- **Quadriceps wasting** → disuse atrophy from chronic pain.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Hip OA**: presents as groin, gluteal, or referred knee pain – often missed because patient points to “hip” but actually feels pain in lateral thigh or knee. Key clue: pain on internal rotation + limited range.
- **Hand OA (erosive)**: rapid onset of swelling, erythema, and tenderness at DIP/PIP joints – mimics inflammatory arthritis. Look for Heberden nodes and central erosions on X-ray.
- **Spinal OA (facet joint)**: lower back pain radiating to buttock/thigh without true radiculopathy; worse with extension, better with flexion. Often confused with disc disease or spinal stenosis.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Osteoarthritis (OA) is a “wearing out” of the joint’s shock-absorbing cartilage, causing pain, stiffness, and loss of movement. As cartilage thins, the underlying bone reacts by forming spurs (osteophytes) and hardening (sclerosis). It is the most common form of arthritis, affecting > 30% of people over 65.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency**: almost every clinic you’ll see OA patients – knee, hip, hand, spine. It is a leading cause of GP visits and orthopaedic referrals.
- **Stakes**: poorly managed OA → chronic pain, reduced mobility, falls, social isolation, and increased surgical demand for joint replacement.
- **Common pitfalls**:  
  - Dismissing “wear and tear” as harmless – it is progressive and treatable.  
  - Misdiagnosing as rheumatoid arthritis (RA) when morning stiffness is short and inflammatory markers are normal.  
  - Over-reliance on X-ray severity: pain and function correlate poorly with radiographic findings; treat the patient, not the film.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of development:**  
OA begins with mechanical stress (obesity, malalignment, trauma) that exceeds the chondrocytes’ capacity to repair. Chondrocytes produce matrix (collagen, proteoglycans) but also release proteolytic enzymes (MMPs, aggrecanases) that degrade it. Eventually, matrix loss exceeds synthesis → cartilage fissures and thins. As cartilage disappears, the joint space narrows. The subchondral bone experiences increased load → micro-fractures, sclerosis, and cysts (geodes). The bone tries to stabilise the joint by forming osteophytes at the margins – these are your **bony swellings**. The synovium may become mildly inflamed (synovitis), causing **effusion** but not florid redness/warmth – that’s why OA is “non-inflammatory” in the classic sense.

**How key signs arise:**
- **Pain**: source is multifactorial – bone stress (periosteal stretching from osteophytes, micro-fractures in subchondral bone), capsular distension, and mild synovitis. Pain is worse with activity because loading increases intraosseous pressure and shear across damaged cartilage.
- **Stiffness**: due to joint effusion and capsular thickening; short because after a few movements the synovial fluid redistributes and capsular tension relaxes.
- **Crepitus**: rough cartilage surfaces rubbing together; may also be from loose bodies (chondral fragments) clicking.
- **Deformity**: medial or lateral joint space collapse → varus (knee bowing) or valgus. In hands: DIP Heberden nodes (osteophytes at terminal phalanx base) and PIP Bouchard nodes.
- **Restricted movement**: capsular fibrosis and osteophyte block. Loss of full extension is an early sign in knee OA.

**Pathophys → pattern recognition cues:**
- Age + obesity + mechanical history → cumulative damage exceeds repair.
- Short morning stiffness → no significant immune complex deposition (unlike RA).
- No warmth → limited cytokine-driven synovitis; if warm, consider inflammatory flare or crystal arthritis.
- Bony enlargement (osteophytes) → load redistribution attempt.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Examples | Stem clue pointing to it |
|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Idiopathic (most common)** | Age, genetic susceptibility, female sex | “No obvious cause in a 70-year-old woman” |
| **Mechanical overload** | Obesity, repetitive high-impact sport, malalignment (genu varum/valgum) | “BMI 38, and a history of marathon running” |
| **Post-traumatic** | Previous fracture, meniscectomy, ACL tear, dislocation | “Knee pain started 10 years after tibial plateau fracture” |
| **Metabolic / deposition** | Hemochromatosis (shoulder/knee), acromegaly (large hands), ochronosis (dark urine, spine) | “Unexplained OA in 50-year-old male with bronze skin and diabetes” |
| **Inflammatory secondary** | Post-septic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, crystal arthropathies → secondary OA | “History of RA now with mechanical pain and X-ray showing joint space narrowing” |
| **Congenital/developmental** | Hip dysplasia, slipped capital femoral epiphysis | “Young adult with groin pain and shallow acetabulum on X-ray” |

**Highlight:** Idiopathic + obesity + prior joint trauma are the triad you’ll see most often in clinic.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic approach:**  
OA is a clinical diagnosis – typical history + exam is enough. Imaging and bloods are used to confirm severity, exclude mimics, and plan surgery.

**American College of Rheumatology (ACR) criteria for knee OA (clinical + radiographic)**  

| Criterion | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| Knee pain | Most days of the prior month |
| Age > 50 | – |
| Morning stiffness < 30 min | – |
| Crepitus on active motion | – |
| Bony tenderness | At joint margins |
| Bony enlargement | – |
| No palpable warmth | – |
| ESR < 40 mm/hr (if drawn) | – |
| X-ray findings | Osteophytes, joint space narrowing, sclerosis, cysts |

Three of: knee pain + age > 50 + stiffness < 30 + crepitus + bony enlargement + no warmth gives 95% specificity.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Plain X-ray (weight-bearing)** | Joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, geodes | Confirm diagnosis, stage severity, plan replacement | *Narrowing*: cartilage loss – medial > lateral in varus OA. *Osteophytes*: pathognomonic for OA. *Sclerosis*: bone response to increased load. *Geodes*: subchondral cysts |
| **ESR / CRP** | Normal or mildly elevated (< 40) | Rule out inflammatory / septic arthritis | Markedly elevated (> 60) suggests infection or inflammatory arthritis |
| **Joint aspiration (if effusion + warmth)** | Clear, straw-coloured fluid; WBC < 2000, no crystals, no organisms | Exclude septic arthritis, pseudogout, gout | Turbid fluid, WBC > 50,000, or crystals = alternative diagnosis |
| **MRI** | Cartilage defects, meniscal tears, subchondral oedema | Not routine; only if atypical pain, suspected osteonecrosis, or pre-op planning | Bone marrow oedema correlates with pain but is often incidental |

**Pattern recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out**
- **Confirms**: X-ray showing **osteophytes** plus **joint space narrowing** in a patient with typical history – stop further workup.
- **Rules out**: **Warm, red, acutely swollen joint** → not OA; aspirate urgently for septic arthritis.
- **Mimic trick**: normal X-ray does NOT rule out OA if symptoms are early; MRI may show cartilage damage.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **Analgesia**: Paracetamol 1 g PO (consider weight-based 10–15 mg/kg). Avoid NSAIDs in undifferentiated pain; if suspicion of crystal arthritis or septic, hold NSAIDs until diagnosis clear.
- **Ice** if acute flare (valgus stress, overactivity) – apply 15 min every 2 hours.
- **Rest + elevation** if weight-bearing is painful – but do not immobilise for > 24h.
- **Why**: paracetamol is safest first-line; ice reduces synovitis; rest prevents further cartilage shear.

**Ward (24–72 h):**  
- **Oral NSAIDs** if no contraindications: Ibuprofen 400 mg TDS with food + PPI (omeprazole 20 mg OD) for gastroprotection. Alternative: Naproxen 500 mg BD.
- **Topical NSAID** (diclofenac gel 1%) for single joint – reduces systemic side effects.
- **Physiotherapy** – quadriceps strengthening, patellar mobilisation, gait retraining. Aim to unload the affected compartment (e.g., lateral wedge insole for medial knee OA).
- **Intra-articular steroid**: Triamcinolone acetonide 40 mg + 1% lidocaine 1 mL, injected into the joint. Reserve for acute effusion or severe pain not controlled by oral meds. Max 3–4 injections per year to avoid accelerating cartilage loss.
- **Why**: NSAIDs alleviate pain and synovitis; steroid injection gives rapid but temporary relief; physiotherapy addresses muscle weakness – the only disease-modifying non-surgical intervention.

**Long-term (3+ months):**  
- **Weight loss**: target 5–10% of body weight → decreases knee load by 2–4× per kilogram lost. Most effective non-pharmacological treatment.
- **Exercise**: low-impact (cycling, swimming, walking) daily for 30 min + strengthening exercises. Avoid high-impact running.
- **Glucosamine / chondroitin**: weak evidence, but some patients respond. Try for 3 months; if no benefit, stop.
- **Assistive devices**: walking stick in opposite hand (reduces hip/knee load by 50%).
- **Surgery referral**: when pain interferes with daily living despite conservative measures – total knee arthroplasty (TKA). For unicompartmental OA, consider partial knee replacement or osteotomy.
- **What NOT to do**:  
  - Do not prescribe opioids first-line – risk of dependency outweighs benefit in chronic OA.
  - Do not give long-term oral steroids – not indicated.
  - Do not delay surgery for “too old”; age alone is not a contraindication.
  - Do not inject steroid into a joint with any sign of infection (redness, fever, high CRP).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Osteoarthritis | Rheumatoid Arthritis | Gout (acute) | Pseudogout (CPPD) | Septic Arthritis |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|--------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Pain** | Gradual, mechanical (worse with activity, better with rest) | Persistent, morning stiffness > 60 min, improves with use | Acute, excruciating, red hot joint – wakes from sleep | Acute or subacute, often knee or wrist | Rapid (hours), severe, any joint – patient looks systemically unwell |
| **Exam** | Bony enlargement, crepitus, no warmth | Soft, boggy synovitis, symmetric PIP/MCP, ulnar deviation | Erythema, warmth, swelling, extreme tenderness | Warm, tender, +/- effusion | Red, hot, swollen, excruciating range of motion |
| **Morning stiffness** | < 30 min | > 60 min | – | – | – |
| **Joints involved** | Knee, hip, DIP, PIP, thumb base, spine | PIP, MCP, MTP, wrist, symmetrical | 1st MTP (podagra), midfoot, knee | Knee, wrist, shoulder | Usually monoarticular (knee, hip) |
| **Radiology** | Osteophytes, joint space narrowing, sclerosis | Erosions (marginal), periarticular osteopenia, symmetric joint loss | Erosions with overhanging edges (rat bites) | Chondrocalcinosis (linear calcification in meniscus/hyaline cartilage) | Soft tissue swelling, possibly later destruction |
| **Labs** | ESR/CRP normal or mildly ↑; no autoantibodies | ↑ CRP, ESR; RF+, anti-CCP+ | ↑ Uric acid (serum > 8.0 mg/dL often) | Normal Ca/PTH; CPPD crystals on aspirate | ↑ WBC, ESR, CRP; aspirate WBC > 50,000, culture+ |
| **Key differentiator** | Pain on weight-bearing, short stiffness, osteophytes | Chronic symmetrical synovitis, RA serologies | Acute monoarthritis, hyperuricemia, urate crystals | Chondrocalcinosis, CPPD crystals, sometimes older age | Fever, septic appearance, purulent aspirate – aspirate before imaging |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complications and how they present:**

- **Disabling chronic pain & immobility**  
  - Presentation: patient gradually becomes housebound, develops deconditioning, muscle atrophy, falls.  
  - Key signs: inability to walk one block, use of walking frame, fear of falling.  
  - Do: refer for surgical opinion – quality of life matters.

- **Joint deformity & instability**  
  - Presentation: severe varus/valgus knee with lateral collateral ligament laxity; hip OA with flexion contracture.  
  - Signs: fixed flexion deformity (loss of full extension) – early clue for severe OA.  
  - Do: consider osteotomy in younger patients, arthroplasty in older.

- **Osteonecrosis** (rare, but can occur in shoulder/hip)  
  - Presentation: sudden worsening of pain, especially at night or at rest, in a previously controlled OA joint.  
  - Signs: MRI shows avascular necrosis.  
  - Do: stop NSAIDs (may contribute), refer for core decompression or arthroplasty.

- **Secondary synovitis / pseudogout flare**  
  - Presentation: acute red, warm, swollen joint in a patient with known OA – mimics septic arthritis.  
  - Signs: effusion, mild temperature, but no systemic shock.  
  - Do: aspirate joint to check crystals and rule out infection.

**Late presentation patterns:**  
If OA is untreated, you see the “end-stage” patient:  
- Knee: severe varus/valgus, 20° fixed flexion, palpable osteophytes the size of marbles, quadriceps barely firing.  
- Hip: short leg (due to superior migration of femoral head), antalgic gait, positive Trendelenburg.  
- Hand: large Heberden and Bouchard nodes with angular deformity, weak grip.  
- These patients are usually referred for arthroplasty – but delaying too long worsens outcomes (muscle wasting prolongs recovery).
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **OA symptoms** – *“C.O.L.D.”*  
  - **C**repitus  
  - **O**ver 50 years  
  - **L**oss of joint space  
  - **D**eformity (bony enlargement)

- **X-ray findings** – *“S.O.C.K.”*  
  - **S**ubchondral sclerosis  
  - **O**steophytes  
  - **C**ysts (geodes)  
  - **K**artilage loss / joint space narrowing

- **NSAID contraindications** – *“B.A.S.H.”*  
  - **B**leeding risk (warfarin, coagulopathy)  
  - **A**sthma (aspirin-sensitive)  
  - **S**tomach ulcer history  
  - **H**ypertension, Heart failure, Hepatic/renal impairment

**Clinical pearls:**

1. **Hip pain ≠ hip disease**: referred pain to the groin, knee, or greater trochanter – examine hip’s internal rotation (first movement lost in OA).
2. **Quadriceps wasting** is an early sign of knee OA – test by looking at the vastus medialis; if flat, the joint is likely compromised.
3. **Patellofemoral OA** presents with anterior knee pain when descending stairs or rising from a chair; X-ray skyline view shows patellar spurring.
4. **Erosive hand OA** can be confusing – it causes swelling and redness but X-ray shows central erosions (not marginal like RA); morning stiffness still short.
5. **Always aspirate a warm effusion** in OA – even if the patient is afebrile, septic arthritis can supervene.
6. **Weight loss > exercises > drugs** – tell patients that losing 5 kg has the same analgesic effect as a 50 mg NSAID.
7. **Post-meniscectomy patients** develop OA faster – counsel them on quad strengthening and low-impact activities.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Ordering MRI for every knee pain – rarely changes management in straightforward OA.
- Giving oral NSAIDs without PPI in elderly – GI bleed risk is high.
- Assuming “normal” X-ray = no OA – early cartilage loss is invisible until narrowing occurs.
- Labeling all joint pain in an elderly person as OA – always check for septic arthritis if acute and warm.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior / orthopaedics / ICU:**

| Condition | Triggers | Action |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| **Suspected septic arthritis** | Acute monoarticular red, hot, swollen joint + fever + raised CRP | **Immediate orthopaedic referral** for aspiration and IV antibiotics (flucloxacillin + gentamicin). Do not wait for X-ray. |
| **Acute on chronic OA with systemic instability** | Joint effusion + NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially lactate > 2, HR > 110, systolic < 90) | Sepsis evaluation – call ICU or med reg. |
| **Unable to weight-bear** | Sudden inability to bear weight after minimal trauma – consider pathological fracture (e.g., osteonecrosis) | X-ray + ortho consult. |
| **Intractable pain despite maximal oral analgesia** | VAS 8-10, no relief with paracetamol + NSAID + weak opioid | Discuss with rheumatology or ortho for joint injection or earlier surgical referral. |
| **Compartment syndrome** (rare, after trauma) | Pain out of proportion, tense swelling, paraesthesia, pulselessness | Emergency fasciotomy – call ortho and theatre. |
| **NEWS2 thresholds** | 0–4: routine care. 5–6: urgent review. ≥ 7: immediate escalation to crash team. | In OA, elevation commonly from acute sepsis (uric acid / infected joint). |

**Key specific thresholds:**
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** with an acute hot joint → assume septic until proven.
- **GCS drop** with uncontrolled pain (opioid toxicity) → naloxone, stop opioids.
- **Creatinine rise > 50%** on NSAID → stop NSAID, check fluid status.

> **Final thought:** OA is often dismissed as “just wear and tear,” but mismanagement leads to needless suffering and earlier surgery. Master the pattern: mechanical pain, short stiffness, bony enlargement, normal labs. Use conservative measures aggressively, and know exactly when to escalate for septic arthritis – it’s the one diagnosis you cannot afford to miss.

rawMarkdown:
## Osteoarthritis
**One-liner:** Degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive cartilage loss and bony overgrowth, most common in elderly and obese, leading cause of chronic disability worldwide.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:**  
A 68-year-old woman with a BMI of 34 presents with gradual-onset right knee pain over 2 years. Pain is worse with walking, climbing stairs, and at the end of the day. She has “start-up” stiffness lasting about 10 minutes in the morning. On examination, you find bony enlargement at the medial joint line, palpable crepitus during flexion‑extension, mild effusion, and quadriceps wasting. She has a slight varus deformity. There is no warmth or erythema.

**Dissected pattern clues:**
- **Age > 60** → cartilage senescence + cumulative mechanical stress; OA peaks after 60.
- **Gradual onset** → chronic degenerative process, not acute inflammatory.
- **Activity-related pain** → mechanical loading exacerbates cartilage friction.
- **Short morning stiffness (< 30 min)** → distinguishes from inflammatory arthritis (RA > 60 min).
- **Bony enlargement** → osteophytes (bone spurs) at joint margins.
- **Crepitus** → rough articular surfaces grinding; felt or heard during movement.
- **Varus deformity** → medial compartment collapse from asymmetric cartilage loss.
- **No warmth/erythema** → absent systemic inflammation; OA is ‘cold’ arthritis.
- **Quadriceps wasting** → disuse atrophy from chronic pain.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Hip OA**: presents as groin, gluteal, or referred knee pain – often missed because patient points to “hip” but actually feels pain in lateral thigh or knee. Key clue: pain on internal rotation + limited range.
- **Hand OA (erosive)**: rapid onset of swelling, erythema, and tenderness at DIP/PIP joints – mimics inflammatory arthritis. Look for Heberden nodes and central erosions on X-ray.
- **Spinal OA (facet joint)**: lower back pain radiating to buttock/thigh without true radiculopathy; worse with extension, better with flexion. Often confused with disc disease or spinal stenosis.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain-language definition:**  
Osteoarthritis (OA) is a “wearing out” of the joint’s shock-absorbing cartilage, causing pain, stiffness, and loss of movement. As cartilage thins, the underlying bone reacts by forming spurs (osteophytes) and hardening (sclerosis). It is the most common form of arthritis, affecting > 30% of people over 65.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency**: almost every clinic you’ll see OA patients – knee, hip, hand, spine. It is a leading cause of GP visits and orthopaedic referrals.
- **Stakes**: poorly managed OA → chronic pain, reduced mobility, falls, social isolation, and increased surgical demand for joint replacement.
- **Common pitfalls**:  
  - Dismissing “wear and tear” as harmless – it is progressive and treatable.  
  - Misdiagnosing as rheumatoid arthritis (RA) when morning stiffness is short and inflammatory markers are normal.  
  - Over-reliance on X-ray severity: pain and function correlate poorly with radiographic findings; treat the patient, not the film.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**Story of development:**  
OA begins with mechanical stress (obesity, malalignment, trauma) that exceeds the chondrocytes’ capacity to repair. Chondrocytes produce matrix (collagen, proteoglycans) but also release proteolytic enzymes (MMPs, aggrecanases) that degrade it. Eventually, matrix loss exceeds synthesis → cartilage fissures and thins. As cartilage disappears, the joint space narrows. The subchondral bone experiences increased load → micro-fractures, sclerosis, and cysts (geodes). The bone tries to stabilise the joint by forming osteophytes at the margins – these are your **bony swellings**. The synovium may become mildly inflamed (synovitis), causing **effusion** but not florid redness/warmth – that’s why OA is “non-inflammatory” in the classic sense.

**How key signs arise:**
- **Pain**: source is multifactorial – bone stress (periosteal stretching from osteophytes, micro-fractures in subchondral bone), capsular distension, and mild synovitis. Pain is worse with activity because loading increases intraosseous pressure and shear across damaged cartilage.
- **Stiffness**: due to joint effusion and capsular thickening; short because after a few movements the synovial fluid redistributes and capsular tension relaxes.
- **Crepitus**: rough cartilage surfaces rubbing together; may also be from loose bodies (chondral fragments) clicking.
- **Deformity**: medial or lateral joint space collapse → varus (knee bowing) or valgus. In hands: DIP Heberden nodes (osteophytes at terminal phalanx base) and PIP Bouchard nodes.
- **Restricted movement**: capsular fibrosis and osteophyte block. Loss of full extension is an early sign in knee OA.

**Pathophys → pattern recognition cues:**
- Age + obesity + mechanical history → cumulative damage exceeds repair.
- Short morning stiffness → no significant immune complex deposition (unlike RA).
- No warmth → limited cytokine-driven synovitis; if warm, consider inflammatory flare or crystal arthritis.
- Bony enlargement (osteophytes) → load redistribution attempt.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Examples | Stem clue pointing to it |
|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Idiopathic (most common)** | Age, genetic susceptibility, female sex | “No obvious cause in a 70-year-old woman” |
| **Mechanical overload** | Obesity, repetitive high-impact sport, malalignment (genu varum/valgum) | “BMI 38, and a history of marathon running” |
| **Post-traumatic** | Previous fracture, meniscectomy, ACL tear, dislocation | “Knee pain started 10 years after tibial plateau fracture” |
| **Metabolic / deposition** | Hemochromatosis (shoulder/knee), acromegaly (large hands), ochronosis (dark urine, spine) | “Unexplained OA in 50-year-old male with bronze skin and diabetes” |
| **Inflammatory secondary** | Post-septic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, crystal arthropathies → secondary OA | “History of RA now with mechanical pain and X-ray showing joint space narrowing” |
| **Congenital/developmental** | Hip dysplasia, slipped capital femoral epiphysis | “Young adult with groin pain and shallow acetabulum on X-ray” |

**Highlight:** Idiopathic + obesity + prior joint trauma are the triad you’ll see most often in clinic.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic approach:**  
OA is a clinical diagnosis – typical history + exam is enough. Imaging and bloods are used to confirm severity, exclude mimics, and plan surgery.

**American College of Rheumatology (ACR) criteria for knee OA (clinical + radiographic)**  

| Criterion | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| Knee pain | Most days of the prior month |
| Age > 50 | – |
| Morning stiffness < 30 min | – |
| Crepitus on active motion | – |
| Bony tenderness | At joint margins |
| Bony enlargement | – |
| No palpable warmth | – |
| ESR < 40 mm/hr (if drawn) | – |
| X-ray findings | Osteophytes, joint space narrowing, sclerosis, cysts |

Three of: knee pain + age > 50 + stiffness < 30 + crepitus + bony enlargement + no warmth gives 95% specificity.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Plain X-ray (weight-bearing)** | Joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, geodes | Confirm diagnosis, stage severity, plan replacement | *Narrowing*: cartilage loss – medial > lateral in varus OA. *Osteophytes*: pathognomonic for OA. *Sclerosis*: bone response to increased load. *Geodes*: subchondral cysts |
| **ESR / CRP** | Normal or mildly elevated (< 40) | Rule out inflammatory / septic arthritis | Markedly elevated (> 60) suggests infection or inflammatory arthritis |
| **Joint aspiration (if effusion + warmth)** | Clear, straw-coloured fluid; WBC < 2000, no crystals, no organisms | Exclude septic arthritis, pseudogout, gout | Turbid fluid, WBC > 50,000, or crystals = alternative diagnosis |
| **MRI** | Cartilage defects, meniscal tears, subchondral oedema | Not routine; only if atypical pain, suspected osteonecrosis, or pre-op planning | Bone marrow oedema correlates with pain but is often incidental |

**Pattern recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out**
- **Confirms**: X-ray showing **osteophytes** plus **joint space narrowing** in a patient with typical history – stop further workup.
- **Rules out**: **Warm, red, acutely swollen joint** → not OA; aspirate urgently for septic arthritis.
- **Mimic trick**: normal X-ray does NOT rule out OA if symptoms are early; MRI may show cartilage damage.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**  
- **Analgesia**: Paracetamol 1 g PO (consider weight-based 10–15 mg/kg). Avoid NSAIDs in undifferentiated pain; if suspicion of crystal arthritis or septic, hold NSAIDs until diagnosis clear.
- **Ice** if acute flare (valgus stress, overactivity) – apply 15 min every 2 hours.
- **Rest + elevation** if weight-bearing is painful – but do not immobilise for > 24h.
- **Why**: paracetamol is safest first-line; ice reduces synovitis; rest prevents further cartilage shear.

**Ward (24–72 h):**  
- **Oral NSAIDs** if no contraindications: Ibuprofen 400 mg TDS with food + PPI (omeprazole 20 mg OD) for gastroprotection. Alternative: Naproxen 500 mg BD.
- **Topical NSAID** (diclofenac gel 1%) for single joint – reduces systemic side effects.
- **Physiotherapy** – quadriceps strengthening, patellar mobilisation, gait retraining. Aim to unload the affected compartment (e.g., lateral wedge insole for medial knee OA).
- **Intra-articular steroid**: Triamcinolone acetonide 40 mg + 1% lidocaine 1 mL, injected into the joint. Reserve for acute effusion or severe pain not controlled by oral meds. Max 3–4 injections per year to avoid accelerating cartilage loss.
- **Why**: NSAIDs alleviate pain and synovitis; steroid injection gives rapid but temporary relief; physiotherapy addresses muscle weakness – the only disease-modifying non-surgical intervention.

**Long-term (3+ months):**  
- **Weight loss**: target 5–10% of body weight → decreases knee load by 2–4× per kilogram lost. Most effective non-pharmacological treatment.
- **Exercise**: low-impact (cycling, swimming, walking) daily for 30 min + strengthening exercises. Avoid high-impact running.
- **Glucosamine / chondroitin**: weak evidence, but some patients respond. Try for 3 months; if no benefit, stop.
- **Assistive devices**: walking stick in opposite hand (reduces hip/knee load by 50%).
- **Surgery referral**: when pain interferes with daily living despite conservative measures – total knee arthroplasty (TKA). For unicompartmental OA, consider partial knee replacement or osteotomy.
- **What NOT to do**:  
  - Do not prescribe opioids first-line – risk of dependency outweighs benefit in chronic OA.
  - Do not give long-term oral steroids – not indicated.
  - Do not delay surgery for “too old”; age alone is not a contraindication.
  - Do not inject steroid into a joint with any sign of infection (redness, fever, high CRP).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Osteoarthritis | Rheumatoid Arthritis | Gout (acute) | Pseudogout (CPPD) | Septic Arthritis |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|--------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Pain** | Gradual, mechanical (worse with activity, better with rest) | Persistent, morning stiffness > 60 min, improves with use | Acute, excruciating, red hot joint – wakes from sleep | Acute or subacute, often knee or wrist | Rapid (hours), severe, any joint – patient looks systemically unwell |
| **Exam** | Bony enlargement, crepitus, no warmth | Soft, boggy synovitis, symmetric PIP/MCP, ulnar deviation | Erythema, warmth, swelling, extreme tenderness | Warm, tender, +/- effusion | Red, hot, swollen, excruciating range of motion |
| **Morning stiffness** | < 30 min | > 60 min | – | – | – |
| **Joints involved** | Knee, hip, DIP, PIP, thumb base, spine | PIP, MCP, MTP, wrist, symmetrical | 1st MTP (podagra), midfoot, knee | Knee, wrist, shoulder | Usually monoarticular (knee, hip) |
| **Radiology** | Osteophytes, joint space narrowing, sclerosis | Erosions (marginal), periarticular osteopenia, symmetric joint loss | Erosions with overhanging edges (rat bites) | Chondrocalcinosis (linear calcification in meniscus/hyaline cartilage) | Soft tissue swelling, possibly later destruction |
| **Labs** | ESR/CRP normal or mildly ↑; no autoantibodies | ↑ CRP, ESR; RF+, anti-CCP+ | ↑ Uric acid (serum > 8.0 mg/dL often) | Normal Ca/PTH; CPPD crystals on aspirate | ↑ WBC, ESR, CRP; aspirate WBC > 50,000, culture+ |
| **Key differentiator** | Pain on weight-bearing, short stiffness, osteophytes | Chronic symmetrical synovitis, RA serologies | Acute monoarthritis, hyperuricemia, urate crystals | Chondrocalcinosis, CPPD crystals, sometimes older age | Fever, septic appearance, purulent aspirate – aspirate before imaging |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
**Major complications and how they present:**

- **Disabling chronic pain & immobility**  
  - Presentation: patient gradually becomes housebound, develops deconditioning, muscle atrophy, falls.  
  - Key signs: inability to walk one block, use of walking frame, fear of falling.  
  - Do: refer for surgical opinion – quality of life matters.

- **Joint deformity & instability**  
  - Presentation: severe varus/valgus knee with lateral collateral ligament laxity; hip OA with flexion contracture.  
  - Signs: fixed flexion deformity (loss of full extension) – early clue for severe OA.  
  - Do: consider osteotomy in younger patients, arthroplasty in older.

- **Osteonecrosis** (rare, but can occur in shoulder/hip)  
  - Presentation: sudden worsening of pain, especially at night or at rest, in a previously controlled OA joint.  
  - Signs: MRI shows avascular necrosis.  
  - Do: stop NSAIDs (may contribute), refer for core decompression or arthroplasty.

- **Secondary synovitis / pseudogout flare**  
  - Presentation: acute red, warm, swollen joint in a patient with known OA – mimics septic arthritis.  
  - Signs: effusion, mild temperature, but no systemic shock.  
  - Do: aspirate joint to check crystals and rule out infection.

**Late presentation patterns:**  
If OA is untreated, you see the “end-stage” patient:  
- Knee: severe varus/valgus, 20° fixed flexion, palpable osteophytes the size of marbles, quadriceps barely firing.  
- Hip: short leg (due to superior migration of femoral head), antalgic gait, positive Trendelenburg.  
- Hand: large Heberden and Bouchard nodes with angular deformity, weak grip.  
- These patients are usually referred for arthroplasty – but delaying too long worsens outcomes (muscle wasting prolongs recovery).

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**

- **OA symptoms** – *“C.O.L.D.”*  
  - **C**repitus  
  - **O**ver 50 years  
  - **L**oss of joint space  
  - **D**eformity (bony enlargement)

- **X-ray findings** – *“S.O.C.K.”*  
  - **S**ubchondral sclerosis  
  - **O**steophytes  
  - **C**ysts (geodes)  
  - **K**artilage loss / joint space narrowing

- **NSAID contraindications** – *“B.A.S.H.”*  
  - **B**leeding risk (warfarin, coagulopathy)  
  - **A**sthma (aspirin-sensitive)  
  - **S**tomach ulcer history  
  - **H**ypertension, Heart failure, Hepatic/renal impairment

**Clinical pearls:**

1. **Hip pain ≠ hip disease**: referred pain to the groin, knee, or greater trochanter – examine hip’s internal rotation (first movement lost in OA).
2. **Quadriceps wasting** is an early sign of knee OA – test by looking at the vastus medialis; if flat, the joint is likely compromised.
3. **Patellofemoral OA** presents with anterior knee pain when descending stairs or rising from a chair; X-ray skyline view shows patellar spurring.
4. **Erosive hand OA** can be confusing – it causes swelling and redness but X-ray shows central erosions (not marginal like RA); morning stiffness still short.
5. **Always aspirate a warm effusion** in OA – even if the patient is afebrile, septic arthritis can supervene.
6. **Weight loss > exercises > drugs** – tell patients that losing 5 kg has the same analgesic effect as a 50 mg NSAID.
7. **Post-meniscectomy patients** develop OA faster – counsel them on quad strengthening and low-impact activities.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Ordering MRI for every knee pain – rarely changes management in straightforward OA.
- Giving oral NSAIDs without PPI in elderly – GI bleed risk is high.
- Assuming “normal” X-ray = no OA – early cartilage loss is invisible until narrowing occurs.
- Labeling all joint pain in an elderly person as OA – always check for septic arthritis if acute and warm.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call your senior / orthopaedics / ICU:**

| Condition | Triggers | Action |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| **Suspected septic arthritis** | Acute monoarticular red, hot, swollen joint + fever + raised CRP | **Immediate orthopaedic referral** for aspiration and IV antibiotics (flucloxacillin + gentamicin). Do not wait for X-ray. |
| **Acute on chronic OA with systemic instability** | Joint effusion + NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially lactate > 2, HR > 110, systolic < 90) | Sepsis evaluation – call ICU or med reg. |
| **Unable to weight-bear** | Sudden inability to bear weight after minimal trauma – consider pathological fracture (e.g., osteonecrosis) | X-ray + ortho consult. |
| **Intractable pain despite maximal oral analgesia** | VAS 8-10, no relief with paracetamol + NSAID + weak opioid | Discuss with rheumatology or ortho for joint injection or earlier surgical referral. |
| **Compartment syndrome** (rare, after trauma) | Pain out of proportion, tense swelling, paraesthesia, pulselessness | Emergency fasciotomy – call ortho and theatre. |
| **NEWS2 thresholds** | 0–4: routine care. 5–6: urgent review. ≥ 7: immediate escalation to crash team. | In OA, elevation commonly from acute sepsis (uric acid / infected joint). |

**Key specific thresholds:**
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** with an acute hot joint → assume septic until proven.
- **GCS drop** with uncontrolled pain (opioid toxicity) → naloxone, stop opioids.
- **Creatinine rise > 50%** on NSAID → stop NSAID, check fluid status.

> **Final thought:** OA is often dismissed as “just wear and tear,” but mismanagement leads to needless suffering and earlier surgery. Master the pattern: mechanical pain, short stiffness, bony enlargement, normal labs. Use conservative measures aggressively, and know exactly when to escalate for septic arthritis – it’s the one diagnosis you cannot afford to miss.
